name: Kettlebell Bent-Over Reverse Fly
resistance_profile: Free-weight horizontal abduction against gravity — the moment arm and difficulty peak at the top when the arms reach shoulder height, giving a peak-contraction bias.
cues: Lead with the elbows, not the hands.
Keep the neck long and the gaze down.
Think about pouring the pinkies up and out.
execution_steps: Hold a light kettlebell in each hand by the handle and hinge at the hips until the torso is near parallel to the floor.
Let the arms hang beneath the shoulders with a soft, fixed elbow bend, the bells resting toward the wrists.
Raise the kettlebells out to the sides in a wide arc, leading with the elbows and pinkies.
Stop when the upper arms reach shoulder height and briefly squeeze the rear delts.
Lower under control back to the hanging start without rounding the spine.
good_when: Kettlebells are your available free weight and you want strict rear-delt isolation.
You want peak-contraction emphasis to complement cable or stretch-biased work.
You can hold a stable, braced hip hinge for the set.
bad_when: You cannot keep a neutral spine in the hinge — use a chest-supported version instead.
You specifically want a lengthened-biased stimulus (choose the cable fly).
common_errors: Using momentum or jerking the torso upward.
Shrugging or rowing instead of flying out to the sides.
Rounding the lower back in the hinge.
progressions: Add reps, then move to a slightly heavier kettlebell.
Add a top-position pause for extra peak contraction.
regressions: Support the chest on an incline bench to remove the hinge.
Reduce the load or switch to dumbbells for an easier grip.
